question_en: Which statements about complexity classes are correct? I. The graph path decision problem CAM is in P. II. A problem X is NP-complete if X is in NP and every problem in NP reduces to X in polynomial time. III. If a decision problem X is in P, then its complement is in NP.
explanation_en: Reachability is decidable in polynomial time, the NP-complete definition is correct, and P is closed under complement; therefore the complement is also in P and hence in NP.

question_en: Which statements about decision problems and NP-completeness are correct? I. Decision problems ask yes/no questions, while location and optimization problems ask for structures or optimal structures. II. NP-completeness is studied through decision problems. III. NP-complete problems are among the hardest problems in NP, and a polynomial-time algorithm for one NP-complete problem would imply polynomial-time algorithms for all problems in NP.
explanation_en: The statements match the standard framing of NP-completeness: it is defined for decision problems, and polynomially solving one NP-complete problem would solve all NP problems in polynomial time.

question_en: Which statements about formal languages, automata, computability, and NP-completeness are correct?
explanation_en: The official key marks III and IV. The NP-related point is that NP-complete problems are a subset of NP.
